title: Galerkin-finite difference method for fractional parabolic partial differential equations
author: Hossan, Datta, et al.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: We provide the first Bernoulli polynomials and their modified versions to use as basis functions for the weighted residual methods throughout this paper.

steps.title: Galerkin method for space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: We solve the Eq. (3) with the help of the Galerkin Weighted Residual method. In this method, basis functions need to meet the homogeneous boundary requirements. And initial condition is another important step in solving any kind of partial differential equation. In this case, we select initially a trial solution. Let us consider such an approximate solution of the Eq. (3) as(5)uËœ(x,Ï„)=Î¦0(x,Ï„)+âˆ‘j=1naj(Ï„)Î¦j(x) where Î¦0(x,Ï„) satisfies the non-homogeneous part of the boundary conditions, Î¦j(x) denotes the basis functions which must fulfill the homogeneous boundary requirements, and the unknown parameters, aj(Ï„) are function of time [44].

steps.title: Galerkin method for space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: Now the residual function is(6)R(x,Ï„)=âˆ‚uËœâˆ‚Ï„âˆ’q(x)âˆ‚Î²uËœâˆ‚xÎ²âˆ’s(x,Ï„)Substituting the Eq. (5) into (6) we get,(7)âˆ‚uËœâˆ‚Ï„=âˆ‘j=1ndaj(Ï„)dÏ„Î¦j(x)+âˆ‚Î¦0(x,Ï„)âˆ‚Ï„and(8)âˆ‚Î²uËœâˆ‚xÎ²=âˆ‘j=1naj(Ï„)dÎ²Î¦j(x)dxÎ²+âˆ‚Î²Î¦0(x,Ï„)âˆ‚xÎ²Then the weighted residual equation is(9)âˆ«01R(x,Ï„)Î¦i(x)dx=0where, Î¦i(x) are the basis functions defined in (2) for all i=1,2,...,n.

steps.title: Galerkin method for space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: Substituting (7) and (8) into Eq. (6), then (9) leads us,(10)âˆ«01(âˆ‘j=1ndaj(Ï„)dÏ„Î¦j(x)+âˆ‚Î¦0(x,Ï„)âˆ‚Ï„âˆ’q(x)(âˆ‘j=1naj(Ï„)dÎ²Î¦j(x)dxÎ²+âˆ‚Î²Î¦0(x,Ï„)âˆ‚xÎ²)âˆ’s(x,Ï„))Î¦i(x)dx=0

steps.title: Galerkin method for space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: Now(11)âˆ‘j=1ndaj(Ï„)dÏ„âˆ«01Î¦j(x)Î¦i(x)dx+âˆ«01âˆ‚Î¦0(x,Ï„)âˆ‚Ï„Î¦i(x)dxâˆ’âˆ‘j=1naj(Ï„)âˆ«01(dÎ²Î¦j(x)dxÎ²)Î¦i(x)q(x)dxâˆ’âˆ«01(âˆ‚Î²Ï•0(x,Ï„)âˆ‚xÎ²)Î¦i(x)q(x)dxâˆ’âˆ«01s(x,Ï„)Î¦i(x)dx=0 which can be rewritten as(12)âˆ‘j=1ndaj(Ï„)dÏ„âˆ«01Î¦j(x)Î¦i(x)dxâˆ’âˆ‘j=1naj(Ï„)âˆ«01(dÎ²Î¦j(x)dxÎ²)Î¦i(x)q(x)dx=âˆ«01(âˆ‚Î²Î¦0(x,Ï„)âˆ‚xÎ²)Î¦i(x)q(x)dxâˆ’âˆ«01âˆ‚Î¦0(x,Ï„)âˆ‚Ï„Î¦i(x)dx+âˆ«01s(x,Ï„)Î¦i(x)dxWe can define the integrals of the above equation in terms of matrix. The dimension of the matrix depends on the number of parameters or basis functions. In matrix notation, the Eq. (12) reduces to(13)[C]{da(Ï„)dÏ„}+[K]{a(Ï„)}={F}where,Cij=âˆ«01Î¦j(x)Î¦i(x)dxKij=âˆ’âˆ«01(âˆ‚Î²âˆ‚xÎ²Î¦j(x))Î¦i(x)q(x)dxFi=âˆ«01(âˆ‚Î²Î¦0(x,Ï„)âˆ‚xÎ²q(x)âˆ’âˆ‚Î¦0(x,Ï„)âˆ‚Ï„+s(x,Ï„))Î¦i(x)dx

steps.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: This method is a combination of discretization of time fractional terms [34], and the modified Galerkin method for space fractional terms. Now we are ready to provide the mathematical formulation for the Eq. (4) explicitly. For this, we introduce the discretized approximation of the time-fractional derivative [34] as:âˆ‚Î±u(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ‚Ï„Î±=(Î´Ï„)âˆ’Î±Î“(2âˆ’Î±){u(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ’u(x,Ï„n)+âˆ‘l=1n(u(x,Ï„n+1âˆ’l)âˆ’u(x,Ï„nâˆ’l))((l+1)(1âˆ’Î±)âˆ’l(1âˆ’Î±))} which is equivalent to,(14)âˆ‚Î±u(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ‚Ï„Î±=V[u(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ’u(x,Ï„n)+âˆ‘l=1n(u(x,Ï„n+1âˆ’l)âˆ’u(x,Ï„nâˆ’l))Pl]where, Pl=((l+1)(1âˆ’Î±)âˆ’l(1âˆ’Î±)), V=(Î´Ï„)âˆ’Î±Î“(2âˆ’Î±) and time step size as, Î´Ï„=Ï„N

steps.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: After applying Galerkin method, we calculate u(x,Ï„n) at different values of x, and at any specified time step, Ï„n which is defined for n=1,2,...,N.

steps.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: We choose the approximate solution as,(15)uËœ(x,Ï„n)=Î¦0(x,Ï„n)+âˆ‘i=1kai(Ï„n)Î¦i(x)where, j represents the number of parameters. Unlike as Galerkin method, Î¦0(x,Ï„n) calculates the non-homogeneous part of the boundary conditions at any specified value of time, Ï„n,ai(Ï„n) are the unknown parameters whose values are needed to be determined again at the specified time, Ï„n and Î¦i(x) are basis functions such as we have discussed in (2). Now we define the residual function asR(x,Ï„n+1)=V[uËœ(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ’uËœ(x,Ï„n)+âˆ‘l=1n(uËœ(x,Ï„n+1âˆ’l)âˆ’uËœ(x,Ï„nâˆ’l))Pl]âˆ’q(x)[âˆ‚Î²uËœ(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ‚xÎ²]âˆ’s(x,Ï„n+1)To find a set of linear equations, we consider:(16)âˆ«01R(x,Ï„n+1)Î¦j(x)dx=0wherej=1,2,...,kor equivalently,(17)âˆ«01(V[uËœ(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ’uËœ(x,Ï„n)+âˆ‘l=1n(uËœ(x,Ï„n+1âˆ’l)âˆ’uËœ(x,Ï„nâˆ’l))Pl]âˆ’q(x)[âˆ‚Î²uËœ(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ‚xÎ²]âˆ’s(x,Ï„n+1))Î¦j(x)dx=0

steps.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: After substitution of uËœ from Eq. (15) into Eq. (17) we get,âˆ«01(V[(Î¦0(x,Ï„n+1)+âˆ‘i=1kai(Ï„n+1)Î¦i(x))âˆ’(Î¦0(x,Ï„n)+âˆ‘i=1kai(Ï„n)Î¦i(x))+âˆ‘l=1n((Î¦0(x,Ï„n+1âˆ’l)+âˆ‘i=1kai(Ï„n+1âˆ’l)Î¦i(x))âˆ’(Î¦0(x,Ï„nâˆ’l)+âˆ‘i=1kai(Ï„nâˆ’l)Î¦i(x)))Pl]âˆ’q(x)[âˆ‚Î²(Î¦0(x,Ï„n+1)+âˆ‘i=1kai(Ï„n+1)Î¦i(x))âˆ‚xÎ²]âˆ’s(x,Ï„n+1))Î¦j(x)dx=0or,V[âˆ«01((Î¦0(x,Ï„n+1)+âˆ‘i=1kai(Ï„n+1)Î¦i(x))Î¦j(x))dxâˆ’âˆ«01((Î¦0(x,Ï„n)+âˆ‘i=1kai(Ï„n)Î¦i(x))Î¦j(x))dx+âˆ‘l=1n(âˆ«01((Î¦0(x,Ï„n+1âˆ’l)+âˆ‘i=1kai(Ï„n+1âˆ’l)Î¦i(x))Î¦j(x))dxâˆ’âˆ«01((Î¦0(x,Ï„nâˆ’l)+âˆ‘i=1kai(Ï„nâˆ’l)Î¦i(x))Î¦j(x))dx)Pl]âˆ’âˆ«01q(x)[âˆ‚Î²(Î¦0(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ‚xÎ²+âˆ‘i=1kai(Ï„n+1)âˆ‚Î²(Î¦i(x))âˆ‚xÎ²]Î¦j(x)dxâˆ’âˆ«01s(x,Ï„n+1)Î¦j(x)dx=0 or,(18)V[âˆ«01((Î¦0(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ’Î¦0(x,Ï„n))Î¦j(x)dx+âˆ‘i=1k(ai(Ï„n+1)âˆ’ai(Ï„n))âˆ«01Î¦i(x)Î¦j(x)dx+âˆ‘l=1n(âˆ«01((Î¦0(x,Ï„n+1âˆ’l)âˆ’Î¦0(x,Ï„nâˆ’l))Î¦j(x)dx+âˆ‘i=1k(ai(Ï„n+1âˆ’l)âˆ’ai(Ï„nâˆ’l))Ã—âˆ«01Î¦i(x)Î¦j(x)dx)Pl]âˆ’âˆ«01q(x)(âˆ‚Î²(Î¦0(x,Ï„n+1))âˆ‚xÎ²)Î¦j(x)dxâˆ’âˆ‘i=1kai(Ï„n+1)Ã—âˆ«01q(x)âˆ‚Î²(Î¦i(x))âˆ‚xÎ²Î¦j(x)dxâˆ’âˆ«01s(x,Ï„n+1)Î¦j(x)dx=0

steps.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: We evaluate the values of unknown parameters, ai(Ï„n) after substituting the value of n in Eq. (18). For example, if we substitute n=0, we obtain linear equations at Ï„=Î´Ï„. Solving those equations we find out an approximate solution at a specified time value as a function of x such as uËœ(x,Î´Ï„). Repeating the steps for n=1,2,...,N, we will be able to calculate the values of u(x,Ï„) at different x for any value of time. We express the integrals in terms of matrix and in matrix notation,(19)V[[G]{a(Ï„n+1)âˆ’a(Ï„n)}+âˆ‘l=1n({H}+[G]{a(Ï„n+1âˆ’l)âˆ’a(Ï„nâˆ’l)})Pl]=[K]{a(Ï„n+1)}+{M}whereGij=âˆ«01Î¦i(x)Î¦j(x)dxHj=âˆ«01((Î¦0(x,Ï„n+1âˆ’l)âˆ’Î¦0(x,Ï„nâˆ’l))Î¦j(x)dxKij=âˆ«01q(x)(âˆ‚Î²âˆ‚xÎ²Î¦i(x))Î¦j(x)dxMj=âˆ«01(q(x)âˆ‚Î²(Î¦0(x,Ï„n+1))âˆ‚xÎ²âˆ’V(Î¦0(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ’Î¦0(x,Ï„n))+s(x,Ï„n+1))Î¦j(x)dx

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: To simplify the convergence analysis of the Galerkin-Finite Difference method, we consider the following fractional diffusion equation in both space and time but without a source term and a constant diffusion coefficient (K).(20)âˆ‚Î±uâˆ‚Ï„Î±=Kâˆ‚Î²uâˆ‚xÎ²If we write the residual function setting to zero we get,(21)V[uËœ(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ’uËœ(x,Ï„n)+âˆ‘i=1n(uËœ(x,Ï„n+1âˆ’i)âˆ’uËœ(x,Ï„nâˆ’i))pi]=K[âˆ‚Î²uËœ(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ‚xÎ²]Particularly, for n=0, (Ï„1=Î´t) the Eq. (21) can be written as,(22)uËœ(x,Ï„1)âˆ’KV[âˆ‚Î²uËœ(x,Ï„1)âˆ‚xÎ²]=p0uËœ(x,Ï„0)and, in general, we get the following equation,(23)uËœ(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ’KV[âˆ‚Î²uËœ(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ‚xÎ²]=(1âˆ’p1)uËœ(x,Ï„n)+pnuËœ(x,Ï„0)+âˆ‘i=1nâˆ’1(piâˆ’pi+1)uËœ(x,Ï„nâˆ’i)

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Let us introduce error as, Ïµ(xm,Ï„n)=Ïµmn=umnâˆ’uËœmn with Ïµn=(Ïµ1n,Ïµ2n,...,ÏµMâˆ’1n)T and Ïµ0=0. In our study we introduce, âˆ‚Î²âˆ‚xÎ²=DÎ². After substitution of the approximate solution into Eq. (22) and (23) we get,Ïµm1âˆ’KV[DÎ²(Ïµm1)]=Ïµm0+Rm1and,Ïµmn+1âˆ’KV[DÎ²(Ïµmn+1)]=(1âˆ’p1)Ïµmn+pnÏµm0+âˆ‘i=1nâˆ’1(piâˆ’pi+1)Ïµmnâˆ’i+Rmn+1In this case, the value of m remains the same but n varies from 1 to Nâˆ’1. And we are going to use, |Rmn|â‰¤C(Î´Ï„)where,n=1,2,...,Nandm=1,2,...,Mâˆ’1

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Now, through letting âˆ¥Ïµ1âˆ¥âˆž=|Ïµj1|where,xj=max(xm), we can write,|Ïµj1|â‰¤|Ïµj1|âˆ’KV[DÎ²(|Ïµj1|)]â‰¤|Ïµj1âˆ’KV[DÎ²(Ïµj1)]|=|Ïµj0+Rj1|Since, Ïµ0=0and,|Rj1|â‰¤C(Î´Ï„)then,âˆ¥Ïµ1âˆ¥âˆžâ‰¤C(Î´Ï„) For the next part of the proof we consider the following:âˆ¥Ïµiâˆ¥âˆžâ‰¤Cpiâˆ’1âˆ’1(Î´Ï„),piâˆ’1â‰¥pnâˆ’1andâˆ¥Ïµn+1âˆ¥âˆž=|Ïµjn+1|;where,xj=max(xm).Now,|Ïµjn+1|â‰¤|Ïµjn+1|âˆ’KV[DÎ²(|Ïµjn+1|)]â‰¤|Ïµjn+1âˆ’KV[DÎ²(Ïµjn+1)]|=|(1âˆ’p1)Ïµjn+pnÏµj0+âˆ‘i=1nâˆ’1(piâˆ’pi+1)Ïµjnâˆ’i+Rjn+1|â‰¤(1âˆ’p1)|Ïµjn|+âˆ‘i=1nâˆ’1(piâˆ’pi+1)|Ïµjnâˆ’i|+|Rjn+1|â‰¤{(1âˆ’p1)pnâˆ’1âˆ’1+âˆ‘i=1nâˆ’1(piâˆ’pi+1)pnâˆ’iâˆ’1âˆ’1}C(Î´Ï„)+|Rjn+1|So, âˆ¥Ïµn+1âˆ¥âˆžâ‰¤pnâˆ’1{(1âˆ’p1)+âˆ‘i=1nâˆ’1(piâˆ’pi+1)+pn}C(Î´Ï„)=pnâˆ’1C(Î´Ï„). Therefore, the convergence order of the scheme is given by|uËœmnâˆ’u(xm,Ï„n)|â‰¤C(Î´Ï„)where,n=1,2,...,Nandm=1,2,...,Mâˆ’1.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Throughout this, we have exploited C as a positive constant with (0.5<C<2) depending on the problem. The following theorem is derived for the case, 0<Î±<1. Based on the above discussion and literature [[34], [45]], we can state the following similar theorem:

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Theorem: Consider the approximate solution as uËœ(xm,Ï„n) of the exact solution u(xm,Ï„n), and for simplification, we choose, uËœ(xm,Ï„n)=uËœmn and u(xm,Ï„n)=umn. Then there is always a positive constant C such that,|uËœmnâˆ’umn|â‰¤C(Î´Ï„)where,n=1,2,...,Nandm=1,2,...,Mâˆ’1.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: For stability analysis, we insert (14) into (20) to get(24)V[u(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ’u(x,Ï„n)+âˆ‘i=1n(u(x,Ï„n+1âˆ’i)âˆ’u(x,Ï„nâˆ’i))pi]=K[âˆ‚Î²u(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ‚xÎ²]On using (15), (21) and (24) we can write(25)V[u(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ’uËœ(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ’(u(x,Ï„n)âˆ’uËœ(x,Ï„n))+âˆ‘i=1n(u(x,Ï„n+1âˆ’i)âˆ’uËœ(x,Ï„n+1âˆ’i)âˆ’(u(x,Ï„nâˆ’i)âˆ’uËœ(x,Ï„nâˆ’i)))pi]=K[âˆ‚Î²u(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ‚xÎ²âˆ’âˆ‚Î²uËœ(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ‚xÎ²]

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Let us now define the error term at n-th time step as:Emn=u(xm,tn)âˆ’uËœ(xm,tn)where,n=0,1,2,...,Nandm=0,1,2,...,M.Inserting the above error term in Eq. (25) for any m-th space step we can write,(26)V[Emn+1âˆ’Emn+âˆ‘i=1n(Emn+1âˆ’iâˆ’Emnâˆ’i)pi]=Kâˆ‚Î²âˆ‚xÎ²(Emn+1).Now for n=0, the Eq. (26) becomes(27)V[Em1âˆ’Em0]=Kâˆ‚Î²âˆ‚xÎ²(Em1).We consider Lâˆž norm of the error defined as âˆ¥E1âˆ¥âˆž=|Ej1|,wherexj=max(xm), and (27) leads us(28)âˆ¥E1âˆ¥âˆž=|Ej1|â‰¤|Ej1|âˆ’KV(âˆ‚Î²âˆ‚xÎ²|Ej1|)=|Ej0|â‰¤âˆ¥E0âˆ¥âˆž.For n=1, the Eq. (26) reduces to(29)V[Em2âˆ’Em1+(Em1âˆ’Em0)p1)]=Kâˆ‚Î²âˆ‚xÎ²(Em2).Using the notation âˆ¥E2âˆ¥âˆž=|Ej2|, we obtain.(30)âˆ¥E2âˆ¥âˆž=|Ej2|â‰¤|Ej2|âˆ’KV(âˆ‚Î²âˆ‚xÎ²|Ej2|)=|(1âˆ’p1)Ej1+p1Ej0|â‰¤(1âˆ’p1)|Ej0|+p1|Ej0|â‰¤âˆ¥E0âˆ¥âˆžSimilarly, by using method of induction we can show that for any l-th time step,(31)âˆ¥El+1âˆ¥âˆžâ‰¤âˆ¥E0âˆ¥âˆžThus, we may conclude that the finite difference of the time fractional term is unconditionally stable, and details are available in [34].

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Now, we consider four problems, including two applications that appear in Financial Mathematics. Here we define an absolute error, Lâˆž-norm and order of convergence, respectively, as follows,AbsoluteError=|uËœ(x,t)âˆ’u(x,t)|Lâˆž-norm=max(Ai),whereAiaretheabsoluteerrors.OrderofConvergence,C(Î´t)=e1e2where, e1 and e2 represents Lâˆž- norm for different time step size.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Problem-1: We consider the problem defined in Eq. (3) and is taken from [32], on a specified domain, 0 0and,u(x,0)=x3.The exact solution is:u(x,t)=x2(1âˆ’x)eâˆ’tUsing the mathematical formulation described for space fractional PDE with two parameters let us approximate the solution in the form:(33)uËœ(x,t)=âˆ‘i=12ai(t)Î¦i(x).We obtain the following residual function:(34)R(x,t)=âˆ‚uËœ(x,t)âˆ‚tâˆ’(q(x)âˆ‚1.8uËœ(x,t)âˆ‚x1.8+s(x,t))and the residual equation force us(35)âˆ«01R(x,t)Î¦j(x)dx=0where,j=1,2or,âˆ‘i=12dai(t)dtâˆ«01Î¦i(x)Î¦j(x)dxâˆ’âˆ‘i=12ai(t)âˆ«01(d1.8dx1.8Î¦i(x))Î¦j(x)q(x)dxâˆ’âˆ«01s(x,t)Î¦j(x)dx=0In matrix notation, we define,(36)[C]{da(Ï„)dÏ„}+[K]{a(Ï„)}={F}where,Cij=âˆ«01Î¦j(x)Î¦i(x)dxKij=âˆ’âˆ«01(âˆ‚1.8âˆ‚x1.8Î¦j(x))Î¦i(x)q(x)dxFi=âˆ«01s(x,Ï„)Î¦i(x)dx

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Here, Î¦1(x)=x2âˆ’x, Î¦2(x)=x2âˆ’3x22+x3. Hence, we obtain,

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: In [46], shifted Chebyshev polynomials of the second kind were used to solve the problem. Abbas and Mehdi solved the space fractional diffusion equation using a numerical technique based on the shifted Legendre tau method in [30]. In [31], the fractional diffusion equation is reduced to a system of ordinary differential equations by using the properties of Chebyshev polynomials. In [32], the collocation method using the fourth kind of Chebyshev polynomial and compact finite difference method has been used. Now we compute approximate values and absolute errors using two parameters which are displayed in Table 1, Table 2, and Table 3 at different values of time. In these tables, we compare our obtained results with the existing values [30], [31], [32], [46]. These tables show that the Galerkin method provides us with better accuracy for various values of x, and we may observe that the Galerkin approach has outperformed the existing methods described in [30], [31], [32], [46]. steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Problem-2: We are considering the Eq. (4) with time and space fractional diffusion equation as [45]:(37)âˆ‚Î±uâˆ‚tÎ±=âˆ‚Î²uâˆ‚xÎ²+f(x,t) for 0<Î±<1 and 1<Î²<2, andf(x,t)=Î“(3+Î±)2x2(xâˆ’1)t2âˆ’2x(2âˆ’Î²)t(2+Î±)[3xÎ“(4âˆ’Î²)âˆ’1Î“(3âˆ’Î²)],with the boundary and initial conditions: u(0,t)=u(1,t)=0 and u(x,0)=0. The exact solution for this problem is, u(x,t)=x2(xâˆ’1)t(2+Î±)

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Since both time and space contain fractional derivatives we have fixed the values of derivative order such as Î±=0.2andÎ²=1.6 for this problem. To start with the solution to this problem, as basis functions, we have chosen only two modified Bernoulli polynomials. Due to the presence of fractional time derivative, we have solved this problem using the Galerkin- Finite Difference method. At first, we choose an approximate solution for this problem in the following form:(38)uËœ(x,Ï„n)=Î¦0(x,Ï„n)+âˆ‘i=12ai(Ï„n)Î¦i(x) where, Ï•0(x,Ï„) represents non-homogeneous part of our boundary condition and Ï•0(x,Ï„n) displays itâ€™s value at any specified time. Since we already have homogeneous boundary conditions so we can have, Ï•0(x,Ï„n)=0. So, our new approximate solution becomes,(39)uËœ(x,Ï„n)=âˆ‘i=12ai(Ï„n)Î¦i(x)To approximate the solution of Eq. (37) using the Galerkin method we solve the following integration, and obtain a set of linear equations,(40)âˆ«01R(x,Ï„n+1)Î¦j(x)dx=0Putting the residual function into Eq. (40) we getâˆ«01(V[uËœ(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ’uËœ(x,Ï„n)+âˆ‘l=1n(uËœ(x,Ï„n+1âˆ’l)âˆ’uËœ(x,Ï„nâˆ’l))Pl]âˆ’[âˆ‚Î²uËœ(x,Ï„n+1)âˆ‚xÎ²]âˆ’f(x,Ï„n+1))Î¦j(x)dx=0Using Eq. (39) in the above equation we can obtain,V[âˆ‘i=12ai(Ï„n+1)âˆ«01(Î¦i(x)Î¦j(x))dxâˆ’âˆ‘i=12ai(Ï„n)âˆ«01(Î¦i(x)Î¦j(x))dx+âˆ‘l=1n(âˆ‘i=12ai(Ï„n+1âˆ’l)âˆ«01(Î¦i(x)Î¦j(x))dxâˆ’âˆ‘i=12ai(Ï„nâˆ’l)âˆ«01(Î¦i(x)Î¦j(x))dx)Pl]âˆ’âˆ‘i=12ai(Ï„n+1)âˆ«01[âˆ‚Î²(Î¦i(x))âˆ‚xÎ²]Î¦j(x)dxâˆ’âˆ«01(f(x,Ï„n+1)Î¦j(x))dx=0For, n=0 we get two linear equations as following:(41)V[âˆ«01(Î¦12(x))dx(a1(Ï„1)âˆ’a1(Ï„0))+âˆ«01(Î¦1(x)Î¦2(x))dx(a2(Ï„1)âˆ’a2(Ï„0))]=âˆ«01(Î¦12(x))dx(a1(Ï„1))+âˆ«01(Î¦1(x)Î¦2(x))dx(a2(Ï„1))+âˆ«01(f(x,Ï„1)Î¦1(x))dx(42)V[âˆ«01(Î¦1(x)Î¦2(x))dx(a1(Ï„1)âˆ’a1(Ï„0))+âˆ«01(Î¦22(x))dx(a2(Ï„1)âˆ’a2(Ï„0))]=âˆ«01(Î¦1(x)Î¦2(x))dx(a1(Ï„1))+âˆ«01(Î¦22(x))dx(a2(Ï„1))+âˆ«01(f(x,Ï„1)Î¦2(x))dxNow we substitute the values of unknown parameters (a1,a2) at initial time state (Ï„0=0) from the given initial condition via following the steps discussed below:(43)âˆ«01(uËœ(x,0)âˆ’u(x,0))Î¦j(x)dx=0Applying the approximate value from Eq. (39) into (43) we get,(44)âˆ«01(âˆ‘i=12ai(0)Î¦i(x)âˆ’0)Î¦j(x)dx=0From Eq. (44) we get a set of linear equations which provide us the values of unknowns. Repeating the process for increasing time steps we obtain our desired result. For this problem, we take the basis functions as:

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: The solution of this problem is not similar to Eq. (37) due to the discretization of the time-fractional derivative. So, our approximated solution is represented at t=1 as, u(x,1)=1.01x3âˆ’1.02x2+0.0127x and at t=0.5 as, u(x,0.5)=0.219x3âˆ’0.222x2+0.00323x

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Now, we compute absolute error and order of convergence to compare the results with the existing literature [47], which are shown in Tables 4 and 5 for different values of Î± and Î².Table 4Error analysis for Problem-2 for Î±=0.4 and Î²=1.75.Table 4Î´tFor Î´x=11000 in [45]Present method for Î´x=110Absolute ErrorConvergence order C(Î´t)Absolute ErrorConvergence order C(Î´t)1208.535166Ã—10âˆ’05-4.521834Ã—10âˆ’03-1402.901776Ã—10âˆ’051.55653.495112Ã—10âˆ’031.29381809.718379Ã—10âˆ’061.57812.553417Ã—10âˆ’031.368811603.176376Ã—10âˆ’061.61331.804433Ã—10âˆ’031.4151Table 5Error analysis for Problem-2 for Î±=0.5 and Î²=1.88.Table 5Î´tFor Î´x=11000 in [45]Present method for Î´x=110Absolute ErrorConvergence order C(Î´t)Absolute ErrorConvergence order C(Î´t)1201.326761Ã—10âˆ’04-5.600296Ã—10âˆ’03-1404.803713Ã—10âˆ’051.46574.587591Ã—10âˆ’031.22071801.722465Ã—10âˆ’051.47973.550801Ã—10âˆ’031.291911606.114506Ã—10âˆ’061.49422.660682Ã—10âˆ’031.3345

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: To analyze the convergence order we have considered maximum absolute error for different time steps and displayed them in Table 6, and we observe that the order of convergence is ensured. The tabular representation of absolute errors for Problem-2 is shown in Table 7 and Table 8. We generate Table 7 and Table 8 by changing one of the parameters (Î±, Î²) and keeping fixed the other. According to the tables, for fixed Î²=1.6 we get better values of u(x,t) at Î±=0.2. and for fixed Î±=0.5 we get good approximation values of u(x,t) at Î²=1.8.
